title: Type Annotations Basics
description: Explicitly define the types of your variables.

slides.title: Explicit Typing 🏷️
slides.content:
Although TypeScript can often guess or infer types, it is good practice to explicitly define them.

We do this with a **type annotation**.

A type annotation involves placing a colon `:` followed by the type name next to the variable.

slides.title: Adding Annotations
slides.content: Here is how we explicitly type a variable:

quizData.question: Which symbol is used for type annotations in TypeScript?
quizData.explanation: We use a colon to annotate types, like `let name: string`.

slides.title: Uninitialized Variables ⚠️
slides.content:
Type annotations are crucial when you declare a variable but do not immediately assign a value to it.

Without an annotation and without an initial value, TypeScript won't know what type the variable holds.

slides.title: Why Annotate?
slides.content: Without an annotation, TypeScript gives the variable an `any` type, which defeats the purpose of checking types!
